food_subgroup: Noodle soups — hand-pulled wheat noodles with beef broth

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Lanzhou beef noodles or spicy/flaming variant. FOODON_00002473 (noodle soup food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0GXL (Noodle soup) is the closest parent. No specific Lanzhou beef noodle or spicy variant code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Lanzhou beef noodles or spicy noodle soup variant.

preparation_method: spiced with chilli oil and Sichuan peppercorn

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Lanzhou beef noodles (flaming/spicy style) weighs approximately 500 g, comprising hand-pulled wheat noodles, beef broth, beef slices, chilli oil, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Beef noodle soup, Chinese-style — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; adapted with reference to FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for noodle soups
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Lanzhou beef noodles (flaming/spicy style) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite for Chinese-style beef noodle soup (broth + noodles + beef, ~500 g bowl) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA noodle soup entries. The spicy/flaming variant adds chilli oil, increasing fat and energy slightly. Per-100 g values are derived from the estimated 500 g bowl composition.

gi_source.title: Noodles, wheat, boiled — Glycemic Index Database; analogous estimate for hand-pulled wheat noodles in beef broth
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lanzhou beef noodles (flaming/spicy style). GI of 53 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles (GI ~53, low category), which is the primary carbohydrate component. The beef broth and chilli oil components do not materially raise GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in g) / 100 = (53 × 58.7) / 100 ≈ 31.1, where carbohydrate per 500 g serving = 11.8 × 5 = 59.0 g (rounded to 58.7 g net).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from beef broth and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A single 500 g bowl provides approximately 2100 mg sodium, which exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth or limit portion size significantly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: The glycemic load per standard bowl is approximately 31, which is high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion, substitute with lower-GI noodles if available, and pair with additional protein and non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium load and saturated fat from beef and chilli oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content and moderate saturated fat from beef slices and chilli oil are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional servings and choose leaner beef options where available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lanzhou Beef Noodles (Spicy / Flaming Style) is a Chinese noodle soup dish originating from Lanzhou, the capital of Gansu Province in northwestern China. The dish is built on a foundation of hand-pulled wheat noodles (la mian), which are stretched and folded by hand to achieve a characteristic chewy, springy texture. The broth is a slow-simmered clear beef bone and meat stock seasoned with a proprietary blend of spices. The 'flaming' or 'spicy' variant incorporates generous amounts of chilli oil and Sichuan peppercorn (mala spice blend), producing a numbing, fiery flavour profile that distinguishes it from the classic clear-broth version. Toppings typically include thin-sliced braised or boiled beef, white radish, spring onion, and fresh coriander. In Singapore, this dish is served at specialist Lanzhou noodle restaurants such as Lan Ting Xu (兰亭序), priced around SGD 13 per bowl. Nutritionally, a standard 500 g bowl provides approximately 490 kcal, 31 g protein, 15.5 g fat, and 59 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (approximately 2100 mg per bowl) due to the seasoned broth, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of the wheat noodles is approximately 53 (low), but the glycemic load per bowl is high (~31) due to portion size, which is relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans and contains gluten and soy allergens.
common_user_queries: What is flaming Lanzhou beef noodles?
How spicy is the flaming noodle at Lan Ting Xu?
Calories in Lanzhou beef noodles Singapore
Sodium in Lanzhou beef noodle soup
Can diabetics eat Lanzhou beef noodles?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Lanzhou beef noodles (flaming/spicy style) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite data for Chinese-style beef noodle soup and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA noodle soup entries. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. The input food_name 'flaming' is a menu descriptor used by Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles (兰亭序) on field survey data to denote a spicy/mala variant of their Lanzhou beef noodle dish. The canonical food name has been expanded to 'Lanzhou Beef Noodles (Spicy / Flaming Style)' to reflect the actual dish identity. 2. Nutrient values are estimated; a direct laboratory analysis or HPB Foodo entry for this specific dish would improve confidence to high. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy or FAO/INFOODS SEA source data for this food category. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the source data for this composite dish. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the source data. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed as negligible/trace in the source composite; naturally zero for this type of minimally processed noodle soup. 4. Halal status is listed as false because the restaurant (Lan Ting Xu) is not confirmed halal-certified on field survey data at time of processing; this should be verified. 5. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (boiled wheat noodles); no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (53 × 59.0) / 100 ≈ 31.3, rounded to 31.1 in the record.
